title: Cat Rapid Shallow Breathing (Tachypnea): Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat breathing fast? Rapid shallow breathing (tachypnea) is a high-priority emergency. Learn what it means and why you must act now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Tachypnea is rapid, shallow breathing in cats and represents a high-priority medical emergency requiring immediate veterinary care. Recording a short video of your cat's breathing can provide crucial diagnostic information for your vet.

What is tachypnea and how does it affect my cat?

Tachypnea is the medical term for a rapid, shallow respiratory rate. In cats, this often manifests as the chest moving up and down very quickly without the mouth necessarily being open. Unlike dogs, cats rarely pant to cool down; therefore, any significant increase in breathing rate usually indicates that the body is struggling to maintain proper oxygen levels or is responding to a severe internal stressor.

Is rapid breathing in my cat a medical emergency?

  • Rapid shallow breathing is a high-priority medical emergency.
  • If your cat is breathing more than 40 times per minute while at rest, or if you notice their sides heaving, it is critical to seek veterinary care immediately.
  • This condition can lead to respiratory failure within minutes or hours depending on the underlying cause, such as fluid in the lungs, heart disease, or feline asthma.

How can taking a video of my cat’s breathing help the veterinarian?

  • If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the vet, capturing a short 10-second video of your cat’s breathing can be life-saving.
  • Cats often change their breathing patterns due to the stress of a car ride or being in a clinic, making a baseline video essential.
  • A video allows the veterinarian to see the exact rhythm and effort of the breathing as it occurred in a relaxed environment, helping them differentiate between cardiac and primary respiratory issues more quickly.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compromised breathing in cats manifests as an increased respiratory rate and effort, followed by changes in respiratory pattern. Cats may sit crouched on all four limbs with the sternum slightly elevated. Obvious labored, open-mouth breathing and cyanosis develop last, indicating significant loss of pulmonary function and impending pulmonary arrest. Pleural space disease causes asynchronous breathing, whereas lung parenchymal disease causes quiet, smooth breathing with the chest and abdominal wall moving in the same direction. Animals with severe small airway obstruction have labored breathing with an expiratory push of the diaphragm, cyanosis, and anxiety; auscultation may reveal high-pitched wheezes throughout the lung field. In severe life-threatening situations, the animal may be cyanotic, open-mouth breathing, collapsed, and asphyxiating, potentially due to anaphylactic reactions, asthma (cats), or bronchial obstruction.

Chapter: Emergency, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1663)

faqs.question: What is a normal resting respiratory rate for a cat?
faqs.answer: A healthy cat should take between 15 and 30 breaths per minute while sleeping or resting quietly.

faqs.question: Can stress cause rapid breathing in cats?
faqs.answer: While stress can increase heart rate, persistent rapid shallow breathing usually indicates an underlying medical crisis that requires professional evaluation.

faqs.question: What are other signs of respiratory distress?
faqs.answer: Look for blue-tinged gums, open-mouth breathing, extended neck, or your cat sitting in a 'hunched' position with elbows flared out.
